topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The turpentine fumes were thick enough to chew, a familiar, suffocating blanket in my studio. I was meticulously applying the final glaze to the cityscape, a piece that had taken me three weeks of agonizing focus. Then I saw it. A subtle shift in the shadow line of the distant spire. It wasn't a flaw in the paint; it was an inked line, impossibly precise, tracing the exact contour of my central figure. My hand froze mid-stroke, the brush hovering inches above the canvas. I blinked, hard, then leaned in, scrutinizing the detail. It was a miniature, perfect rendition of my own hand, rendered in stark blackwork. A slow, tight knot began to tighten in my chest. Who? How? I dropped the palette knife with a clatter that sounded deafening in the quiet room.

[story 2]

"Are you quite finished with that monstrosity?" I asked, my voice tight, gesturing toward the easel with a hand that trembled slightly. Marcus just shrugged, a lazy, infuriating motion. But then my gaze snagged on the lower quadrant of the portrait—the swirling nebula I’d spent days perfecting. Embedded within the cosmic dust, rendered with unnerving realism, was a stylized depiction of my own eye, looking directly out. A sudden, sharp intake of breath hitched in my throat. I snatched a magnifying glass, my knuckles white against the cheap plastic. This wasn't a coincidence. This was deliberate. The realization washed over me like cold water, leaving a residue of pure, simmering annoyance. I slammed the glass onto the table, the sound sharp and final.

[story 3]

I scrubbed a hand across my forehead, the gesture automatic, a reflex against the mounting pressure building behind my eyes. The canvas was drying, the air still and heavy. I walked over to check the drying rack, and there, on the edge of the linen, was a small, almost hidden detail. A delicate, fine-line tattoo, mirroring the exact pattern of the lightning storm I had painstakingly captured in the composition. It was undeniably mine, yet it wasn't *my* work. It was someone else’s signature, pressed into the very fabric of my creation. A low, guttural sound escaped my lips. I stood there, breathing shallowly, my jaw clenching so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. The sheer audacity of it made my stomach churn with a deep, unpleasant feeling.
